Last but not least, Kinsale has only about $225 million in debt. Relative to its $167 million cash position and its $7.5 billion capital, investors do not need to worry about its liquidity, which becomes more relevant when there is an industry downturn or macroeconomic turbulence.

The Downgrade and the Disconnect

As reported here, Jefferies downgraded the stock to an underperform or sell-equivalent rating with a price target of $312 from its previous target of $392, citing growth deceleration, potential margin pressure, and valuation concerns.

This downgrade may be “correct” or at least reasonable for investors with a short-term horizon, as the stock price may continue to slide before the soft insurance market bottoms out.

However, the downgrade occurred after the stock had declined materially, roughly 25% from its recent highs around $400. The analyst was effectively saying that the business fundamentals of the company had deteriorated by the same order of magnitude.

That conclusion feels reactive and mechanical. When the business fundamentals and therefore intrinsic value of an enterprise remain largely intact, the fact that its stock price has declined by as much as 25% or so thanks to the cyclical downturn of the sector it operates in should be a reason to upgrade the stock, not to downgrade it, as the stock now represents a better value proposition, at least that should be true for long-term investors.

A Bipolar Stock Market

The current market environment has a somewhat bipolar quality to it. On one side, there are companies, the haves of the stock market, with strong narratives, particularly in areas like artificial intelligence, where growth is expected and often taken for granted. These companies continue to command high valuations and take up a higher and higher portion of the entire stock market's valuation.

On the other side are the have-nots, the S&P 490 or so, some of which are quietly delivering strong performance but seeing their stocks fall well within the bear market territory. Kinsale falls into this category. To the extent their sector, whether it's homebuilding or insurance, faces any cyclical headwinds, their stock prices may have been disproportionately punished.

I do not mean to imply that the concerns about the insurance cycle or macroeconomic conditions are unfounded. The industry is indeed moving into a softer phase, and pricing pressure is real. Growth is likely to moderate, and margins may face some headwinds over time, and the global and US economies do face geopolitical risks, fiscal deficits, inflation, and other risks. For those reasons, investors should not assume that the current price of Kinsale stock represents the bottom. The cycle may take time to play out, and it is really hard to time the bottom of the market.

However, this is where and when we can selectively and incrementally build long positions in solid companies whose stocks are priced around or below their intrinsic values, so long as we do not see any structural or fundamental deterioration of their actual businesses. It is not catching the proverbial falling knife nor buying on the dip for the sake of buying on the dip.

Risks to My Thesis

There are, of course, risks to my thesis. The insurance market may remain soft for longer than expected, leading to continued pricing pressure and slower growth. In addition, while the stock is cheaper than before, further multiple compression is possible if growth slows more than anticipated and the soft market lasts longer. Finally, although Kinsale has demonstrated strong underwriting discipline, no insurer is immune to unexpected losses, litigation trends, or adverse regulatory developments.

These risks are inherent in the business and must be accepted by any investor in the sector.

Conclusion

The underlying business fundamentals and competitive advantages of Kinsale are not deteriorating, although the company is going through a soft cycle together with the rest of the entire insurance sector. It is adapting to a softer market environment while producing strong profitability and returns.

At the same time, the stock price has declined by about 25%. In my view, while such price decline reflects the insurance sector's short-term trend and the many clouds over the stock market, it has been excessive.

For the reasons above, I am upgrading KNSL to Strong Buy from the previous modest Buy rating. Back in December 2025, I was modestly concerned about its valuation, and such concern has been substantially mitigated. So, I will continue my dollar average approach to increase my holding in Kinsale as the current valuation provides a more attractive entry point.

data.title: In War, Things Tire Or Break, StandardAero To The Rescue
data.description:
"If you can walk away from a landing, it's a good landing. If you can use the airplane the next day, it's an outstanding landing." -- Chuck Yeager

Let’s face it. The MRO (Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul) of aircraft engines and other key components is not the way to start a conversation with someone you just met and would like to impress. Even between two guys just having a beer together, unless they are both engineers, this is a sure way to have fewer friends.

But a $6 billion a year revenue stream from a company that is more than a century old (and recently revitalized), one that is the largest independent MRO firm in the country, and one that is about to enjoy a windfall from all the action already seen from the current administration, can stir even the most jaded investor’s cold heart. I therefore promise to include some history and maxims from pilots and mechanics along the way.

A Brief History of StandardAero

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------In 1898, Samuel Langley, physicist, professor of astronomy, and third secretary of the Smithsonian Institution, began experimenting with a heavier-than-air manned “aerodrome.” On October 3, 1903, he was ready to demonstrate it. Regrettably, it didn’t work. Days later, this editorial appeared: “…the flying machine that will really fly might be evolved by the combined and continuous efforts of mathematicians and mechanicians in from one million to ten million years…” -- New York Times

Two months later, bicycle mechanics Wilbur and Orville Wright developed and flew the first manned airplane.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The predecessor to StandardAero (SARO) was founded in 1911 in Winnipeg, Canada, as Standard Machine Works. Just before WWII, StandardAero was spun off and became fully independent in 1940.

StandardAero was purchased as part of a diversification push by the larger Federal Grain Company in September 1968 (later renamed Federal Industries Ltd.). By the 1970s, StandardAero was overhauling piston engines for airplanes and turboshaft engines for helicopters.

StandardAero was effectively sold four times more until, in 2004, private equity firm The Carlyle Group bought it, only to sell it three years later to Dubai Aerospace Enterprises. StandardAero’s headquarters were moved to Phoenix, Arizona.

SARO is still in Scottsdale today, but it keeps those home fires burning: in partnership with GE Aerospace, it operates the joint Testing, Research, and Development Centre (TRDC) in Winnipeg, which focuses specifically on essential icing certification and cold-weather testing for some of the world’s largest jet engines.

The corporate comings and goings over the years of StandardAero steadily acquiring other firms and being acquired itself could fill a page, and we would all be asleep.

Basically, SARO continued to expand its repertoire of jet engines and other key components, adding, in recent years, the H-60 Black Hawk, performing final assembly in the UK, and the F-16 and other advanced aircraft. Finally, just seventeen months ago, in October 2024, StandardAero became a public company and is now listed on the New York Stock Exchange.

“Flying is the second greatest thrill known to man. Landing is the first."

– attributed to John J. Nance

What Does StandardAero Really DO?

SARO is the largest independent MRO (again, Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul) in the United States. “Independent” means they are not owned or beholden to one aircraft manufacturer or one huge airline company. This means they are not competing with their customers by showing favoritism to one brand.

The US military has fabulous technical training and extensive activities to keep its MRO personnel busy and quite capable. But when a war comes along, the corps of personnel devoted to maintenance and repair (we call them “Maintainers”) is swamped. Just as we don't want to build a church for Easter Sunday, we don’t want so many brilliant (and expensively trained) men and women with nothing to do.

That’s where SARO enters the picture. It is an essential homegrown “ally” keeping the world's most-flown fighter jet, the F-16, and the workhorse (and fun to jump out of) C-130 operational until 2040 and beyond.

If that weren’t enough, StandardAero’s specialty has expanded from numerous older engines to the LEAP, which will be the dominant commercial engine for at least the next 20 years.

"There are three simple rules for making a smooth landing. Unfortunately, no one knows what they are." -- Unknown

The LEAP Engine? What the Heck is That?

The Leading Edge Aviation Propulsion engine is one of the most successful and technologically advanced commercial aviation jet engines in service today. It is lower-carbon and easier on fuel than all its predecessors.

It is built by CFM International, a 50/50 joint venture between GE Aerospace and France’s Safran Aircraft Engines.

The partners each do what they do best. GE is responsible for the high-pressure compressor, combustor, and high-pressure turbine. Safran handles the fan, gearbox, and low-pressure turbine.

The LEAP-1A engine competes head-to-head with Pratt & Whitney’s PW1100G. Both compete for the Airbus A320neo aircraft. LEAP 1B has no competitor. It is the sole supplier to the Boeing 737 MAX family of airplanes. LEAP-1C is built for the COMAC C919. It also has no competitor.

So what is the big deal about these LEAP engines? Among other things…

  • The fan blades are made from a 3D “Woven Carbon Fiber” composite that is lighter and more durable; lighter means less fuel, and more durable means less expensive maintenance.
  • The use of Ceramic Matrix composites in the high-pressure turbines. These “CMCs” can withstand temperatures much higher than those of traditional metal alloys, all the while weighing much less.
  • The fuel nozzles, which in previous versions of other engines required 20 separate parts welded together, are now 3D printed as a single piece. When it comes time to replace them, it takes way less time, and that means way less expense.

Yada, Yada, Yada. What Does This Have to Do with StandardAero?

"Airspeed, altitude, and brains. At least two are always required..."

Source: “The Pilot’s Trinity,” popularized by flight instructors like William K. Kershner, who wrote The Student Pilot’s Flight Manual.

While GE and Safran have MRO capabilities and facilities, other companies must meet significantly stricter requirements to be certified to maintain, repair, and overhaul these engines. It comes down to a small handful of airlines, and, yep, StandardAero leads the list.

From keeping F-16 fighters in the fight to mission-critical military transports, and more recently, helicopters as well, StandardAero is the engine that keeps aviation moving.

As the nation's largest independent MRO, operational readiness is baked into its thinking. We are not talking about Tinkertoys here. You want a hi-tech investment? The intricate engineering involved in maintaining, repairing, and overhauling fast-burner military jets, commercial airlines, helicopters, and private jets – yes, we haven’t mentioned that, but they are a top dog here, too! – “StandardAero is the Standard Bearer” for keeping airplanes where they belong: in the sky, not on the ground.

I have spoken mostly about the essential part of commercial, Bizjet, and military aircraft—the engines—but StandardAero is becoming a “one-stop shop" across the industry.

They now install and repair Avionics such as cockpit displays, flight controls, and navigation systems.

They provide maintenance for the airframe itself, the plane's body, including structural repairs and major modifications.

StandardAero builds the Auxiliary Power Units that provide power while the plane is on the ground.

SARO also has a large, specialized division that repairs individual parts rather than replacing them with expensive new ones. StandardAero’s “Repair-Rather-Than-Replace" technology substantially lowers costs for aircraft operators of all types.

SARO even gets into the interior of the planes, where they start fresh and redesign luxury jet interiors.

Speed, reliability, engineering excellence, and its role as the globe’s largest independent pure-play aerospace engine MRO place StandardAero at the top of the heap.

Valuation

Comparing SARO with its only true US independent competitor, AAR Corp. (AIR), StandardAero sells at a forward PE of about 19, while AAR is projected to be just below that. However, AAR has a $2.5 million market cap, and StandardAero's is more than 3.5 times that. In addition, I see no special catalyst that will significantly change AAR's immediate future revenue, whereas SARO has only this year begun to be involved with the LEAP engine programs. These, I believe, are a major catalyst.

There has been some recent insider selling, but let me put that in context:

Earlier this month, CEO Russell Wayne Ford sold 40,000 shares, but that was a Rule 10b5-1 sale under a plan adopted in August 2025. On the other hand, Independent Director Paul McElhinney bought 37,243 shares in the open market at about $26.41 per share. I interpret that buying with his own money as a sign that this lead director believes the current market price is an attractive entry point.

So is the entry price a good time to consider beginning a position? I think so. SARO is just beginning to generate meaningful revenue from the LEAP engine, and management expects these new endeavors to reach full profitability within the next 90 days.

Finally, SARO management seems to agree with me—the board has now authorized a $450M buyback. Clearly, they believe the stock is priced right, as well!

Risk Factors?

World peace could reign. People could stop flying. Bizjet owners could be shamed by their airplane’s carbon footprint into flying merely First Class on a commercial airline.

Since none of these are likely to occur in our lifetime, SARO could, more realistically, lose its compelling edge by hiring poorly. Or the OEMs (Original Equipment Manufacturers) like GE and Safran could decide they would rather have the expense of establishing scores of maintenance sites around the world, as StandardAero already has.

Of course, there are competitors to consider: other large independent MROs include Germany’s Lufthansa Technik, AAR Corp., Singapore-based ST Engineering, and MTU Aero Engines. Against them, SARO could stumble and lose the current inbred capabilities it enjoys with GE, SAFRAN, the US military, and others.

Supply chain constraints that slow the current pace at which SARO serves its customers could occur, resulting in lower cash flow while waiting for parts.

And these days, there is always the threat of some sort of tariff tirade or a government shutdown. Of course, these would affect all competitors and all end users equally. (As they would, as has been shown most recently, every industry, every sector, and every one of us.)

StandardAero: the nation’s biggest airplane “mechanic.”

StandardAero is a $6 billion company (2025 revenues) with a 15.8% YOY growth profile. The company has over 5,000 commercial, military, and business aviation clients, who are served by 8,000 StandardAero specialists.

The runway for Standard Auto is wide and long. Every 400 hours an F-16 flies, it is taken in for a Phase Inspection. G-forces and supersonic speed take their toll on pilot and aircraft. Maintainers strip off over 100 panels to check for hairline structural cracks, hydraulic leaks, and engine wear that aren't visible during daily pre-flight checks.

Every 1,000 to 2,000 hours, an F-16 engine typically requires a "Hot Section Inspection" (HSI), during which high-heat components such as turbine blades are inspected or replaced. And every 3,000-4,000 hours of use, the jet goes to a "Depot"—a massive facility like those run by StandardAero.

When it comes to maintenance and engineering, every pilot knows that he is a partner with those on the ground who keep them flying.

"I really believe that more than half the battle of flying is not in the air, but on the ground in the hangar." — Beryl Markham, the first person to fly solo across the Atlantic from east to west (1936).

Of course, that doesn’t mean there isn’t some good-natured back-and-forth between these two partners. Here are some “pilot versus maintainers” -- possibly fictional–– discussions that flowed from pilot notes after a flight:

Pilot's Note: "Evidence of leak on right main landing gear." Mechanic's Response: "Evidence removed."

Pilot's Note: "Number 3 engine missing." Mechanic's Response: "Engine found on right wing after brief search."

Pilot: "Test flight OK, except auto-land very rough." Mechanic’s Response: “There is no auto-land on this aircraft.”

--- Primary Source: AviationHumor.net

The Bottom Line

StandardAero is unique. It is not owned by, nor does it have an exclusive single client in the airline business. It is not owned by, nor does it have an exclusive single client of an aircraft manufacturer. The entire aeronautical world is StandardAero’s to prevail or fail. I believe it will prevail.

Respectfully submitted,

Joseph L. Shaefer

data.title: Post-Iran Winners: Oil, Energy, And Israel
data.description:
Equities around the world continue to take it on the chin this March, with month-to-date performance coinciding with the beginning of the start of the war in Iran over the last weekend of February.

Below is a look at our asset class performance matrix featuring total returns for dozens of ETFs across domestic and international equities, sectors, currencies, commodities, and fixed income. For each ETF, we show year-to-date performance through February along with performance so far in March.

ETFs across asset classes are bleeding red this month with the exception of a few: bitcoin (IBIT), the energy sector (XLE), oil (USO) and other energy/ag commodities, and Israel (EIS). Of these month-to-date winners, oil, the agriculture commodities ETF (DBA), the energy sector, and Israel are the only ones that were also up year-to-date before the Iran war began.On the downside, mid-caps and small-caps have been some of the hardest-hit domestic index ETFs, with mid-cap value (IJJ) down the most at -8.1%. Every sector ETF is down this month except energy, with materials down the most at -12%.

Outside the US, country ETFs like France (EWQ), Germany (EWG), India (INDA), Italy (EWI), Japan (EWJ), and Mexico (EWW) are all down 10%+ since the war, while gold (GLD) and silver (SLV) have also gotten pummeled. Additionally, fixed income has not offered any safety due to inflation concerns brought about by the war. The 20+ Year Treasury ETF (TLT) is already down 5.2% since the bombings began.

Below is a look at the recent performance of more than 40 country stock market ETFs traded on US exchanges. On average, these country ETFs were up 9.7% through the first two months of 2026.

Since the end of February, though, they're down an average of 9.7%. Just a few weeks ago, it looked like 2026 was likely to be another year of outperformance for international markets.

Now that there's war in the Middle East and the Strait of Hormuz is unofficially closed for business, countries that heavily rely on energy imports are getting hit (with the exception of Israel). Along with Israel, Norway (ENOR) is the only other country ETF that's positive this month. As a reminder, Norway is one of the few non-Middle Eastern countries that's a net exporter of oil.

Six country ETFs are down 15%+ since the war began: Indonesia (EIDO), Vietnam (VNAM), South Korea (EWY), UAE (UAE), Peru (EPU), and South Africa (EZA).

Of the G7 countries, the US (SPY) is down the least, with a decline of 5.3%, followed by Canada (EWC) - down 8%, and the UK (EWU) - down 9.9%.

data.title: Zillow Home Value Index: 'Real' Home Values Drop To Near 5-Year Low
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Zillow, the real estate listing and brokerage website, provides a wealth of publicly available real estate data. Among these, the Zillow Home Value Index (ZHVI) offers a seasonally adjusted measure of home values and market trends.

Its "flagship" ZHVI is based on data for "all homes, middle tier, smoothed and seasonally adjusted cut," aiming to represent the typical home value rather than the median. Introduced in 2006, the ZHVI includes various segmentations by geography and housing type. Here, we focus on the flagship ZHVI, which reflects typical home values.

Home values continued to trend higher in February, according to the Zillow Home Value Index. However, after adjusting for inflation, real home values continued to drop, remaining at their lowest level in nearly five years. Last month's ZHVI came in at $360,591, up 0.20% from the previous month and up 0.23% from one year ago. After adjusting for inflation, the real figures are -0.03% month-over-month and -2.86% year-over-year.

Zillow Home Value Index Methodology

Zillow’s ZHVI is measured using monthly changes in its “Zestimates,” its proprietary home valuation model. The index is meant to be timely, comprehensive, and transparent, published three weeks after the close of a given month. Many other home value indexes are published with a significant time lag - like the S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller Index and the FHFA House Price Index.

ZHVI is calculated with a few assumptions:

  • Monthly changes are calculated with a weighted mean
  • The average Zestimate within a range determines the index level
  • It can be interpreted as the market’s total appreciation for a given geography or cut

In addition, historical appreciation is based on actual historical housing stock versus a fixed basket of homes, and home improvements are considered in appreciation unrelated to market movements.

The first chart below shows the flagship ZHVI series - the “typical home value” since 2000, not adjusted for inflation. The current nominal value is at an all-time high.

Next, we take a look at the same index values but adjust for inflation using the CPI. As you can see, it’s a different picture. According to the ZHVI, today’s home values are below that of the (inflation-adjusted) housing peak in 2006 and are currently 12.68% off their all-time high from July 2022.

Here is an overlay of the nominal and real indexes for a comparative look.

Housing Prices (ZHVI) and Mortgage Rates

The following charts overlay mortgage rates with both nominal and real ZHVI, highlighting their relationship. Typically, rising interest rates reduce buyer affordability, decreasing demand for homes.

This can lead to downward pressure on home prices. With that said, today’s housing market is following a different path because we’re seeing elevated mortgage rates in conjunction with elevated home prices.

Zillow Home Value Index vs. S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Index

Zillow claims that the ZHVI is “the most accurate and timely measures of residential real estate prices in the United States" and that its index is more representative of housing stock than the S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Index Home Price Index. Zillow claims this is due to its extensive database that includes input from real homeowners as well as their sophisticated home valuation model in the Zestimate.

Zillow believes its ZHVI has distinct advantages over the Case-Shiller index. The Case-Shiller (CS) Index uses the repeat-sales methodology, meaning it measures price changes by looking at homes that have resold in a region, while the ZHVI only includes homes that have sold at least twice in recent history and excludes all new construction.

Here are a few charts pulled directly from a piece by Zillow comparing the two indexes. We tried to get this data to do our own analysis, but the research group there would not provide it. Here’s a link to that article.

© Zillow.com

Another difference between the CS and ZHVI is that CS includes foreclosure resales. Zillow emphasizes that foreclosure resales are “substantially different from non-distressed sales” and are often considerably discounted from such. Zillow claims that using foreclosure resales represents “two very different market segments.” Eric Stubbs, Ph.D. at RBC WM believes foreclosure sales are a matter of taste and says that foreclosures are actual sales, so they do in fact affect the price of surrounding properties.

In any case, the ZHVI is another tool in the tool belt to determine home values and is useful due to its timeliness and variety of information. ETFs associated with residential real estate include iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
